sentences.text: OK.
This was in December, 1930.
I was 14.

And there were constant battles between communists and national socialists in town, and there was also-- started-- they started to bring out the papers, the hate papers, that wanted to destroy Jews, particularly, communists, too, but mostly Jews.
One was called maybe you've heard of it.
And it was full of hatred, full of anti-Semitism.
And so I wrote in that December-- I had actually kept diaries since I was seven years old, but at that time I didn't write about things like that.
And this is December, 1930. 'Often I do not understand mankind.
Maybe I am too young to understand, but maybe grownups have the same problem.
I really believe that humans often don't understand themselves.
They don't know what to make of themselves.
I wish I could live to experience the moment when National Socialists,' in parentheses, 'the swastika bearers, gained insight and learned to recognize how unjust they act.
For they must be unjust.
I can say that in spite of the fact that I'm only 14 years old.
Every just German who does not hate Jews must feel that.
Of course, there are, as in every race or nation Jews who are bad.
But one is correct in saying that Jews have, in many ways, contributed to the German nation and there are intelligent and creative individuals among us Jews.
In war they fought for their fatherland, and many lost their lives on the battlefields.
Many Jews are patriotic, some more so than the Christians.
Also, my father.
We have, anyway, a right to live like the rest of mankind.' And my-- in actuality, my mother lost two first cousins in World War I.
They died for the so-called fatherland.

And then, when I was asked to no longer see my Christian boyfriend, when his mother asked me-- or asked my mother-- to stop having him visit or me visiting their house, then I felt very down and felt, 'everything is struggle, unspeakable, unbearably difficult struggle.
And we have to fight it through.
We have to hold on and continue,' in quotes, 'wound covered.
We, the chosen, the strong sufferers, the bearers of new forces.' And then I wrote a poem.
I have translated the poem.
'Mother, life is too hard to bear.
Mother, I want to give up and cry.
Mother, my strength is no longer there.
Mother, make it easy for me to die.
What is holding me?
Not humanity, not the world, but the all powerful which held together.'
For those of you, written by Goethe, you know.
And I felt that was too much for-- I was 19 at that time.
But I will comment that all my friends had become Nazis.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So that's-- these are things that I had found in-- in my mother's possession.
She had rescued two-- two of my diaries.
She had brought them out of Germany, because we couldn't bring much else.

We brought books and some furniture and-- talking about books, my mother and I-- when we were-- when we were packing the last week when I came home from Switzerland-- we-- my mother and I, with tears in our eyes, we burnt all our books by Russian authors, because we knew that the people-- we had Gestapo, the secret police in the house while we were packing-- that they would think that we were communist if they saw a Russian name like Tolstoy or Dostoevsky.
And so, we would-- something we would never, never, never dream of doing, otherwise, in order to make sure that we could get leave there.
We could leave.
At that point it was critical.
My mother and I burned those books in our own furnace in the house, and it still hurts me when I think of it.
But it was necessary at that time, because these secret police people were there all day.
And when they left in the evening, they sealed the rooms that-- that we were working in, packing in, and we only had the bedrooms and bathrooms to use.
It does.
Oh, yeah.
No.
But the gestapo, these people were pretty ignorant.
Some were undereducated or uneducated, and they wouldn't know the difference, you know?
It was partly what had happened, because I say that in my-- when I describe my best friend and how she left me, or how she wouldn't talk to me anymore in the story-- which I have not yet published.

I have published most of the others-- that I feel at the end there, and I said that I felt-- that I-- it seemed, when I was alone-- going home alone at that time in the street car after our meeting in the woods, 'it seemed an era of my adolescent life had come to an end, and I would have to grow up much faster than I had planned to face the reality of a new and different Germany.' And I locked myself in my room after that because I felt helpless and frustrated.
But I realized that I would not be able to go the normal course of schooling that I had planned, and going to the university afterwards.
At one time, I had thought of becoming a psychiatrist, which, of course, you know, requires medical school and all that.
But I knew that those plans I would not be able to realize then, and I think that had to do with growing up much faster.
Yes.
Mm-hm.
Yes.
I was.
I was close.
I was very close to my mother.
There was a time when I rebelled some, you know, like most teenagers.
Maybe when I was about 13, I remember saying on Mother's Day that I felt I loved her all year round and I didn't feel I should get a present for her just for one day, and things like that.
And she accepted that-- my mother was quite progressive-- and that this was a form of rebellion.
But I was close.
And I was, in some ways, close to my father more, in a different way, of course, you know.
And I looked up to him.
I think he was very knowledgeable.
Even here, he could still speak Latin and Greek.
I mean, he spoke Latin and Greek, but he had Greek more fluently than he spoke English, you know.
So I-- I admired his knowledge.
Oh, I did.
Yes.
Right.
Right.
Yes.
By-- yes, he had.

In 1933, when Hitler came to power on April 1, 1933, my father had a guard posted at his office, at his law practice, and they had pasted 'Jew' over his shingle, so that people, of course, from that time on hesitated, naturally, to go to him.
And it was probably a year or so later that he was told he could no longer go to court.
Well, you can't practice law without going to court.
So that-- he was very limited.
He tried to intervene on behalf of someone he knew who was taken to a concentration camp, and was trying to intervene, and to have this man released.
And he was told, in no uncertain terms, to-- to leave the thing alone and not to interfere.
So he was still practicing in some very limited way.
I don't know exactly what he-- what kind of law where he didn't have to go to court or so.
But by '35, it was certainly obvious that he couldn't make a living anymore.
See?
I see.
So in the interim then, you have gone to England.
What were the circumstances?
I went to England because I could no longer attend school, high school, in Germany.
I actually had only one more year, you know, to finish.
And we decided, my cousin-- the one who later committed suicide-- that we would go together.
She was a year and a half older.
So I was 17, she was but 18 and 1/2.

And we would go and-- well, I spent a few weeks with a family outside of London in Petersfield, where the girl, the daughter, had been a guest with us in Germany for, I think, in the-- during the summer, months at one time.
And so I was there first, and I realized that I wasn't getting anywhere.
You know, I wasn't getting an education anymore.
So we both, my cousin and I, applied to the new school, it's a Steiner school, which I had attended briefly in Germany, too.

And for fixing high tea-- it's a boarding school-- for fixing high tea every day and doing some babysitting for the teacher who owned the home, owned the school, and his wife, we were able to attend classes with the other students.
And we did that for that whole year, and then we went back and my cousin decided, as a Mischling, a mixture, she came from a mixed marriage, that she would be able to stay in Germany.
She felt she was not in imminent danger.
And I knew I couldn't, and I went back to another school in England, in Haslemere.
It's a sort of-- well, it's not London anymore, it's-- I don't-- I can't tell you what direction it was from London.
And this was a school that was established by two German Jewish women teachers so that people could finish their education there.
And I went there.
And then I don't remember what happened in 1935, '36-- end of '35.
I had to leave.
I think it was the English home office didn't give me permission to stay longer.
You have to get permission for some reason.
I don't know.
And I went home again, and then later attended-- beginning of '36-- attended school in Switzerland.
Her name as Edith.
I call her Doris in the story I wrote about her.
Edith.
Coming from a mixed marriage and having been brought up Protestant.
Not really.
I think that really saved her from being-- towards the end of the war, all the-- they started picking up all the people from mixed marriages, too.
And what saved her was that she had developed TB, tuberculosis, and had to go to a sanatorium.
And she was sort of protected there.
I don't know how many knew that she had a Jewish mother, whatever, or how much she talked about it since she didn't want to talk about it when she was younger.
So that protected her.
That was the time when her brother, Gunter, my cousin, first cousin, whom I saw in '89, was in the Hitler Youth.
He was accepted because he was blonde and blue-eyed.
And his mother was-- this was the mother of my cousin Edith-- was hidden in the basement of their home so she would not be deported.
She was scheduled to be deported.
She had her orders.
She had told me that.
First time I went back in 1967, my aunt-- Irma was her name-- told me that she had had her deportation orders.

But her husband, who was faithful to her, protected her-- even though we didn't like the fact that he had Hitler pictures up in his house before we left already-- but he did that in order to protect her, his wife.
So when he had meetings for his colleagues in his house, they thought, you know, it's a nice house with Hitler pictures.
But the daughter, as I said, we were very close at one time.
We had vacations together, my sister and my cousin and I.
I would go with them or she would go with us or whatever.
So but she-- in later years, they did not differentiate anymore, and I knew that.
And I don't know how her brother, Gunter-- the one I saw in '89-- because he won't talk about it.
He wouldn't talk about it.
So I don't know how he was spared from being deported, as well.
She was haunted--
All her life.
And in '86 finally succeeded in committing suicide.
I think she'd had that on her mind for a long time because-- although her mother was dead by then.
But she told me in 1978, when I was back visiting, that I should not tell anyone, any of the neighbors or the store nearby, grocery store, I should not tell them who I was or why I had left Germany.
And I had told her, 33 years after the war ended, how can you still be afraid?
And she said, it's just not a good idea to tell anyone that you're Jewish.
So I didn't.
And she told me not to tell them who my father was.
And I said, nobody would know him any more.
You know, at that point.
During the Holocaust?
Yes.
My mother's sister-- I have a picture of her that you can get later-- was married to an ophthalmologist, and he did not want to leave even though they did have affidavits.
And they had a son in Shanghai, and my uncle did not want to go to Shanghai.
And then we had given them an affidavit, and some relatives, his brother in Philadelphia, gave them an affidavit.
Well, part of it, they did not get out because we did not allow-- under President Roosevelt-- the State Department did not allow, in the early '40s, for any more Jews to come in, any more refugees.
So we found that out much later.
I didn't know why they didn't come.
I have some last letters from them.
But anyway, by aunt was married to this Dr.
Kaufman, Eugene Kaufman, an ophthalmologist.
And they were eventually both deported, after moving, I don't know, X number of times.
And after they had stolen his, apparently, his whole practice-- medical practice-- had been stolen or vandalized, they were deported first to Theresienstadt, which is called Theresien in Polish.
And he apparently died there.
We don't know for sure if he died of starvation or if he was murdered.
And she was sent to Auschwitz and was murdered there.
And we had some other relatives, not as close, I had a second cousin from my father's side who was somewhat younger than I was, and presumably she was one of-- I-- what's his name?
I can't think of-- what's his name?
The doctor?
The famous doctor?
Mengele?
Mengele.
I don't know why-- Eichmann.
I was thinking of Eichmann.
Mengele.
She was one of his-- apparently one of his victims, where he apparently did some surgical procedures on her and then she died.
So there were a number of people.
And there were, of course, many other people that we knew who were exterminated or murdered by the Germans.
Yes.
Yes.
With my aunt, we-- yeah.
I guess it was '45-- but that somebody had known her in the camp and had known that she had been deported to Auschwitz and so on.
And it's documented.
You know, the Germans kept records.
And I went through-- when I was invited back in '89, instead of going to services, I went through the archives at the Jewish Community Centers.
They had preserved the archives from the Nazis.
And the Nazis kept very good track.
And I found out that everybody I knew was listed in there.
Every Jewish person married to a non-Jew, they were all listed there in the records.
It was all in the files.
I looked up all the names I knew from our own-- my mother's three siblings were married to non-Jews, and they were all there.
They knew their birth date, they knew their occupation, they knew their address.
Everything was there for the eventual pick up, or whatever, deportation or whatever they wanted to do--the extermination.
Yes.
No.
My sister was married and was living in Frankfurt.
And her husband had started on [NON-ENGLISH]-- that means he had started training for Israel in-- in agriculture.
And somehow the Germans, of course, they didn't really have to have an excuse, interpreted that as being a communist.
So they withheld the passports of my sister and her husband at the time.
We left in April, '37.
We could still pay for the fare on the And-- by the way, everything else we had to leave at the house, and money, and so on.
We came with a few hundred dollars, I think.
Or a few hundred marks.
But that's why we brought a lot of books-- the books that we could bring, but not much else.
So we left.
And we were, of course, very upset that my sister and her husband could not leave at the same time, and were, you know, naturally trying to-- we gave affidavits.
And we gave affidavits to my mother-- because we didn't have any money, really, we had to ask other people that we had met in St.
Louis to help us with affidavits for my mother's youngest brother, who was in concentration camp.
Although he was married to a non-Jewish woman, too.
And she got him out.
That's his wife, his Christian wife got him out of camp.
But he had to leave within 24 hours.
And he did.
He went to England first, and then came to New York later.
Well, they came out a year and a half later, just before the famous Crystal Night, the Kristallnacht, with a little suitcase and a few dollars or a few marks.
I don't remember what.
They came out with the last train that left from Frankfurt to the border, and they came first to New York and then, for a while, St.
Louis, and then went to Chicago.

They-- He said, my brother-in-law at the time, said that he had a friend, a good Christian friend, who warned him that apparently he had some inkling about Kristallnacht, or that something was going to happen.
He said, get out, get out, something bad is going to happen.
And so they left-- let's see, that was November-- in September or October of '38.
Right.
We came to my relatives.
My mother's oldest brother was the editor-in-chief of the largest German newspaper in this country, the
He was.
Had lived there since before World War I.
Yeah.
Six weeks or so, yeah.
And then came to St.
Louis, because that was supposed to be our permanent residence.
Because we had the relatives who gave us the affidavit there.
Angelica-- it was called Angelica Jacket Company at the time, it was later changed to Angelica Uniform-- they had their headquarters in St.
Louis.
And the people-- the immigration authorities or the Jewish community in New York did not want everybody to stay in New York.
They wanted people to move.
Well, at the time they did not promise that.
They had given the affidavit which meant that they were responsible for us for five years, that we would not become a burden on the government-- on the government.
And, of course, welfare had just started.
There was no real welfare department as we know it now.
And so that's-- when you give an affidavit, when you vouch for somebody, that was the thing that for five years you could not, you know, be supported by anyone, by the governmental authorities or so.
So that was why.
They thought--they were not that anxious to employ my father, but in 1937 there were not many jobs, especially not for lawyers, and he was-- because he didn't practice law anymore here.
That was very hard on him.
It was very hard.
But he-- he got a job as a bookkeeper or something at Angelica.

And I worked, initially, at the Shriners Hospital for Crippled-- it was called for Crippled Children at that time-- as an attendant, 12 hours night, and 12 hours day until I got a scholarship to Washington University, a full scholarship, to go to the School of Occupational Therapy, which I did.
